title: St. Paul the Hermit, St. Longinus, St. Anthony's Vision & St. Eutychius of Rome
narrative:
title": "St. Paul the Hermit, St. Longinus, St. Anthony's Vision & St. Eutychius of Rome

narrative": "title\": \"St. Paul the Hermit, St. Longinus, St. Anthony's Vision & St. Eutychius of Rome

narrative\": \"St. Paul the First Hermit, St. Longinus the Centurion, the Vision of St. Anthony, and St. Eutychius of Rome

On this day the holy Church contemplates the mystery of how God reveals Himself at the extremities of human experience — in the desert cave, on Golgotha, in the ecstatic vision, and in the hidden witness of the Roman city. Saint Paul the First Hermit, the father of all solitaries, had withdrawn into the deep desert of the Thebaid during the Decian persecution and remained there for more than ninety years, nourished by a raven that brought him bread and clothed at last in a garment of woven palm leaves. When the aged Anthony was led by the Spirit to seek him out, he found not a man wasted by deprivation but a person of extraordinary radiance, as though the long solitude had accomplished in him what no communal life could have produced — a complete transparency to the divine light. The Ethiopian tradition receives Paul as the primordial hermit, the one who first demonstrated that the human person, stripped of every worldly support, can live entirely from God.

The vision that Anthony received upon the death of Paul is the second mystery commemorated this day, and it is a wonder that repays prolonged meditation. As Paul breathed his last in his cave, Anthony, journeying toward him from a distance, saw in the spirit the soul of Paul ascending through hosts of angels and prophets and apostles to the very throne of God, shining like the sun. He arrived to find only the body, and knelt beside it in awe, recognising that the whole invisible Church had gathered for this obscure hermit's departure. This vision is preserved in the Ethiopian Synaxarium as a teaching about the magnitude of the hidden life: that Heaven itself assembles in honour of those whom the world has never seen.

Saint Longinus the Centurion stands at the opposite extreme of the world's visibility — he was present at the most public event in human history, the crucifixion of the Lord on Golgotha before the eyes of Jerusalem. It was Longinus who drove the spear into the side of Christ, and according to the tradition preserved in the Synaxarium, drops of the sacred blood and water that flowed from that wound touched his eyes, which had long been failing, and he received his sight. From that moment Longinus could not deny what he had seen and done, and he confessed before all who would hear him that the crucified Man was truly the Son of God. He was later martyred for this confession, sealed by the very blood whose flowing he had occasioned.

Saint Eutychius of Rome is the least celebrated of this day's assembly, a confessor who bore witness to the faith in the city of Rome itself during the persecutions of the early centuries. While the desert fathers withdrew to find God in the silence of the Thebaid, Eutychius remained in the crowded capital of the empire, tending the bodies of martyrs, burying the dead, and ministering to those imprisoned for the faith — an urban ministry of mercy that cost him his own life when the persecutors finally reached him. The Ethiopian Church commemorates him as a witness to the truth that martyrdom and sanctity are not the exclusive province of the desert: God is sought and found at the foot of the Cross, in the sand of the hermit's cave, in the ecstatic vision of the departing soul, and in the alleyways of Rome where the faithful tend the fallen. Amen.
